unit.subject: English
unit.unitTitle: All About Me and My Family
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to your first unit of Year 3 English. In this unit you will learn how to talk about yourself and your family, and about the values that hold a family and a school together: respect, honour, and duty. You will learn how to behave well in public and in private, and how to talk about health and safety. By the end you will introduce your family, ask Wh- questions, give clear instructions, and share what matters to you, just as Amal does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the fifteen Vocabulary words in their three groups. Learn a few words each day.
Work through the six Grammar lessons one at a time, from the present continuous to the joining word and.
Read the five texts in Reading and Listening, ending with the story “Amal's Big Day”, then answer the twelve Comprehension questions.
Work through the four skills in order: Reading and Listening, then Speaking, then Writing, then Activities.
Finish with the Quiz and the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Illustrated children introducing their families and showing kind behaviour in a school hall

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The people who live with you and love you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My family has my mum, my dad, my sister, and me.
practiceSentences: Amal helps her family cook dinner on Saturday mornings.
Sami loves eating dinner with his family every evening.
How many people are in your family?
Nora said, "My family eats together at home."
My family plays board games together on rainy evenings.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - a - m - i - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper listening, name every person in your family and ask the tutor to check each sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A mother or a father who looks after you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My parents take me to school every morning.
practiceSentences: Every parent came to watch our class play.
A parent helps children learn right from wrong.
Which parent walks you to school each day?
Theo said, "My parent packed fresh fruit in my lunch."
I hug each parent before I sleep at night.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - a - r - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor one job each parent does at home, then ask it to add a describing word to your sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person you like and spend time with.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My friend Nora and I play football after school.
practiceSentences: Nora has been my best friend since Year One.
Maya shared her new book with a shy friend.
Who is your best friend at school?
Idris said, "A good friend always tells the truth."
My friend and I walk to the library together.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - i - e - n - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Describe one kind thing a friend did this week, and ask the tutor whether your sentence is clear.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who goes to school to learn new things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Every student in my class can read now.
practiceSentences: A new student joined our class this morning.
Every student lines up quietly before class begins.
Which student watered the class plants today?
The teacher said, "Every student did well today."
I am a proud student at Ehel Academy.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - u - d - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Say three things a good student does each day, and ask the tutor to put them in one long sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A younger person, or someone in a lower class.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My little brother is a junior in the school choir.
practiceSentences: Amal is the junior member of the drama club.
The junior class eats lunch before the older children.
Is your brother a junior in the reading club?
Maya said, "I help the junior pupils find their room."
My cousin is a junior, so I guide him.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: j - u - n - i - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Explain to the tutor, with your helper nearby, how a junior pupil is different from an older pupil.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: An older person, or someone who has learned more.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The senior student helped the younger children find their room.
practiceSentences: Adam is a senior at the big school now.
A senior pupil showed the new children around school.
Will the senior class lead the school assembly?
Theo said, "The senior students read to us on Fridays."
My sister is a senior, so she helps me study.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - n - i - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to give you two sentences about a senior pupil, then say which one sounds kinder.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To act in a good and kind way.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please behave and listen well to your teacher.
practiceSentences: We behave calmly when we visit the town library.
Children who behave well make their teachers happy.
Did you behave kindly at Grandma's house today?
Mum said, "Please behave gently with your baby brother."
I try to behave well at the museum.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - h - a - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor how you behave in the school lunch area, and ask for one more idea to add.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The way a person acts towards other people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: His good behaviour made the whole class happy.
practiceSentences: Kind behaviour helps everyone feel safe at school.
Good behaviour means sharing, listening, and speaking kindly.
Was your behaviour kind during playtime today?
The teacher said, "Your behaviour made me very proud."
My behaviour improves when I remember to be patient.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - h - a - v - i - o - u - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to explain why we say behave for the action and behaviour for the way someone acts.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Being kind to people and listening when they speak.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We show respect when we let other people talk.
practiceSentences: Teacher Yasmin earns our respect by listening carefully.
We show respect by listening when others speak.
How do you show respect to your grandparents?
Maya said, "Respect means waiting quietly for your turn."
I show respect when I greet my teacher politely.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - s - p - e - c - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, list three ways to show respect in class and ask the tutor to rank them.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To show someone great respect for what they do.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We honour our parents by helping them at home.
practiceSentences: We honour Grandma Hana by carrying her heavy bags.
Children honour their parents by obeying them kindly.
How can we honour our grandparents this weekend?
Dad said, "We honour guests by serving them first."
I honour my mother by helping without being asked.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - n - o - u - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor how honour is stronger than respect, then use honour in a sentence about your family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something you must do or take care of.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is my duty to help my little sister with her bag.
practiceSentences: Every duty at home makes my family happier.
Feeding the cat is Sami's duty every morning.
What duty do you have at home this week?
Maya said, "My duty is tidying the bookshelf today."
My duty is setting the table for dinner.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Say one duty you have at home and one at school, and ask the tutor which words made it clear.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How well your body and your mind feel.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Eating fruit and vegetables is good for your health.
practiceSentences: Fresh air and exercise protect our health all year.
Sleeping early keeps your health strong for school.
Why is drinking water good for your health?
The doctor said, "Good food protects your health."
I look after my health by playing outside daily.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - a - l - t - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Tell the tutor what you ate today, then ask which choices were best for your health.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Being safe and not in danger.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We hold hands near the road for our safety.
practiceSentences: The road safety poster hangs beside our classroom door.
Safety comes first when we cross the busy road.
Who teaches you about safety at school?
Dad said, "Hold my hand for your safety."
I follow the safety rules in the science room.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - a - f - e - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper, name three safety rules for the school yard and ask the tutor to check the verbs.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Just for you or a small group, not for everyone.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I write my ideas in my private notebook.
practiceSentences: My private notebook stays inside my school bag.
A diary is private, so we do not read it.
Is your bedroom a private place at home?
Nora said, "My drawings stay private until they are finished."
I keep my private thoughts in a small notebook.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - i - v - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Name one private place in your home, and ask the tutor why we knock before we go inside.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: For everyone to see or use.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The public park is open for all the children to play.
practiceSentences: Omar sells fruit at the busy public market.
The public library is open to everyone in town.
Is the big garden public or just for neighbours?
Sami said, "We keep the public park clean."
My family walks in the public park each evening.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - u - b - l - i - c
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to name five public places, then say how you behave in each one.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about the people in your family and what you do together.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Every family member named in the Speaking 1 recording, plus a complete-sentence answer to comprehension question 1 about Amal's household.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use new words like respect, honour, duty, health, and safety.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: All fifteen unit words sorted and explained in Activity 5, and five of them spelled correctly in the Writing 4 letter.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what is happening now, using the present continuous (“I am helping.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Grammar 1 practice completed with the correct be word, and three accurate is/are + verb-ing sentences in Activity 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask Wh- questions to find out about other people.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three Wh- questions written with question marks in Writing 2, and a recorded question-and-answer turn in Speaking 3.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Give clear instructions using imperatives (“Behave well.”).
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three base-verb instructions produced in Writing 5, and Grammar 3 practice completed with Don't used correctly.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say what you like and don't like, and join ideas with and.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Like and likes used correctly in Speaking 2, and two ideas joined with and in the Writing 6 paragraph.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain how to behave in public and in private places.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: A public example and a private example explained with reasons in comprehension questions 7 and 8, and role-played in Speaking 5.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write simple sentences, questions, a short play, a letter, and instructions.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: All five Writing tasks submitted in the unit portfolio, each checked against the Grade 3 writing rubric.

readings.title: My Family
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: Home, school and town-centre settings familiar to every learner
readings.passageScript: My name is Amal, and I am eight years old. I live with my family in a small house near the town centre. My parents are my mum and my dad. I have an older brother, a younger brother, and a little sister. Grandma Hana lives with us and tells us stories at night. We eat dinner together every night, and we talk about our day. My family helps me, and I help them at home. It is my duty to keep my room tidy and to be kind. I love my family very much.

readings.title: Good Behaviour at School
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: Home, school and town-centre settings familiar to every learner
readings.passageScript: At school, we show respect to our teacher and to our friends. We listen quietly when other people are talking. In a public place, like the lunch area, we sit down calmly. We eat nicely, and we speak softly to the people beside us. Good behaviour keeps everyone safe, calm and happy. We also help the junior students find their classrooms. Our whole school feels like one big family when we honour our teachers.

readings.title: Amal Talks About Her Family
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: Home, school and town-centre settings familiar to every learner
readings.passageScript: “Hello! My name is Amal, and I am a student at Ehel Academy. I live with my parents, my two brothers, my little sister, and my grandmother. My parents are my role models, because they teach me to help other people. It is my duty to honour them and to be a good student.”

readings.title: Public and Private
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: Home, school and town-centre settings familiar to every learner
readings.passageScript: “In private, at home with your family, you can play games and be loud. In public, at school or in the market, you stay calm and speak softly. Behave well, and keep other people safe.”

readings.title: Amal's Big Day
readings.theme: All About Me and My Family
readings.setting: Home, school and town-centre settings familiar to every learner
readings.passageScript:
Everyone in the drama club called Amal “Junior”, because she was the youngest there.
Her older brother Adam had joined the drama club first.
The older students still spoke about him with great respect.
At home, Amal lived with her big, happy family.
Their small house stood near the busy town centre.
Her parents, her brothers Adam and Idris, and her little sister Mina lived there.
Grandma Hana lived with them and told the best stories.
One morning, Amal was getting ready for school.
Her mother said, “Amal, remember to behave in public. Greet your teacher and listen well.”
“I will,” said Amal. “Today our class is doing a play about health and safety.”
Her little sister Mina asked, “What is the play about?”
“It is about staying safe at school and showing respect,” Amal explained.
At school, the students were busy. Some were practising their lines, and some were painting the props.
Amal put on a yellow vest and a paper hat.
She had made the hat herself from an old newspaper.
She whispered to her best friend, Nora, “What is your duty in the play?”
“I am playing the senior student,” said Nora. “You have to teach me how to behave in public and private places.”
“Oh yes!” said Amal. “That is my job today!”
Teacher Yasmin clapped her hands. “Everyone, we are starting now.”
The classroom became quiet, and Amal stepped forward with her chin up.
“Hello, Senior Student!” she said in a big voice. “This is how you behave in the lunch area.”
“Sit down, eat nicely, and speak softly to the people beside you.”
“Why should I do that?” asked Nora, pretending to be rude.
Amal replied, “Because we must show honour and respect to other people.”
“Behave well, and keep everyone around you safe.”
Then Leo walked in and shouted loudly, and the whole class laughed.
Amal smiled and said, “In private, you can be loud with your own family.”
“But in public, we stay calm and we think about other people.”
After the play, Teacher Yasmin smiled. “Who are your role models?” she asked the class.
Amal raised her hand. “My parents,” she said. “They teach me to help others and to be polite.”
“I want to honour them every single day.”
Teacher Yasmin nodded. “That is a wonderful answer, Amal.”
Later that day, Amal saw a junior student drop her bag.
The books and pencils spilled across the ground in front of everybody.
Amal ran over at once. “Let me help you,” she said kindly.
“Thank you!” said the younger girl, smiling. “You are very respectful.”
Amal smiled back and said, “I am still learning, but I am trying.”
On the way home, Amal and Nora walked together along the quiet road.
“I liked your line in the play,” said Nora. “Behave well, and keep everyone safe.”
Amal grinned. “We should make posters for the school corridor,” she said.
“We could write ‘Be safe in public’ or ‘Use kind words every day’.”
“That is a great idea,” said Nora. “Let us ask Teacher Yasmin tomorrow morning.”
That evening at home, Amal was writing carefully in her notebook.
Grandma Hana came in. “What are you doing?” she asked.
“I am writing about my duty,” said Amal. “To help others and to be a good student.”
She showed her grandmother what she had written on the first page.
“At school, I behave with respect. In public, I speak softly.”
“In private, I relax with my family. My duty is to learn, to help, and to stay healthy.”
Her mother looked into the room. “Time for bed,” she said gently.
“All right,” said Amal. “Tomorrow I will keep being my best.”
“Good,” said her mother. “Every day is a new chance to do your duty.”
Amal closed her eyes and smiled, because she liked her nickname now.
It reminded her that she was still learning, but growing up with honour.
One day she would be a senior student that others admired.

comprehension.question: Who lives in the small house with Amal? Name every person.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Her mum, her dad, her two brothers, her little sister, and Grandma Hana.
comprehension.explanation: The second, third, fourth and fifth sentences list the whole household, so all six people must be named.

comprehension.question: What does Amal say her duty at home is?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Her duty is to keep her room tidy and to be kind.
comprehension.explanation: The text gives two parts to the duty, so a full answer names both the tidy room and the kindness.

comprehension.question: Name two ways this text says we show respect at school.
comprehension.correctAnswer: We show respect to our teacher and to our friends. We also listen quietly when other people are talking.
comprehension.explanation: The first two sentences give the two behaviours, so the learner scans the opening lines for them.

comprehension.question: The text calls the lunch area a public place. What does public mean here?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Public means everyone at school shares the area, so we behave calmly there.
comprehension.explanation: The words around it, sit down calmly and speak softly, show that public means shared with other people.

comprehension.question: Who does Amal call her role models, and what do they teach her?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Her parents are her role models, and they teach her to help other people.
comprehension.explanation: The third sentence names the role models and gives the reason in the same breath.

comprehension.question: Amal talks about her grandmother. Should she say he or she about her? Why?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She should say she, because grandmother names one female person.
comprehension.explanation: Choosing the right pronoun depends on who the noun names, which is Grammar Lesson 4 in this unit.

comprehension.question: Where does the speaker say you can play games and be loud?
comprehension.correctAnswer: In private, at home with your family.
comprehension.explanation: The first sentence pairs in private with at home, so the answer must include both parts.

comprehension.question: The speaker names the market as a public place. Why should you speak softly there?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Many other people share the market, so a calm voice keeps everyone safe.
comprehension.explanation: The last line, keep other people safe, gives the reason the learner must join to the market example.

comprehension.question: Why do the drama club students call Amal Junior?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because she is the youngest member of the drama club.
comprehension.explanation: The opening line of the story gives the reason for the nickname straight away.

comprehension.question: What does Amal do straight after she sees the junior student drop her bag?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She runs over at once and offers to help the younger girl.
comprehension.explanation: The sentence beginning Amal ran over at once shows the order of events at the gate.

comprehension.question: How does Amal feel about her nickname by the end? Quote one line that shows it.
comprehension.correctAnswer: She feels proud, and the story says she liked her nickname now.
comprehension.explanation: The feeling must be supported by the closing line, not simply guessed from the happy ending.

comprehension.question: Nora pretends to be rude during the play. Why does she do that?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She is acting the senior student, so Amal can show the polite way.
comprehension.explanation: The play is a lesson inside the story, so Nora's rudeness is acting rather than real behaviour.

grammar.title: Present Continuous (what is happening now)
grammar.explanation: We use the present continuous to talk about actions that are happening right now. It helps us say exactly what people are doing at this moment, like “The student is showing respect.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use two parts: the correct form of be (am / is / are) plus the verb with -ing.
I am + verb-ing: “I am learning English.”
He / She / It is + verb-ing: “My family is talking about safety.”
We / You / They are + verb-ing: “They are helping their parents.”
Spelling tip: if a verb ends in e, drop the e (make → making). If a short verb ends in one vowel and one consonant, double the consonant (run → running).
grammar.commonMistake: Some learners write “They helping their parents.” The be word has gone missing. Put it back: “They are helping their parents.”
grammar.memoryTip: If you can add “right now” to the end of the sentence, the present continuous fits. “She is reading — right now!”
grammar.practice: Write the correct form: use am / is / are and add -ing to the verb in brackets. | The children ______ (play) outside. | She ______ (eat) her lunch. | We ______ (learn) about health. | He ______ (do) his homework. | My family ______ (talk) about safety.

grammar.title: Wh- Questions (asking for information)
grammar.explanation: Wh- questions begin with who, what, where, when, why, and how. Good learners ask questions to find out more about people and about the world around them.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Who → a person: “Who is your best friend?”
What → a thing or an idea: “What is your duty at school?”
Where → a place: “Where do you play after school?”
When → a time: “When do you have lunch?”
Why → a reason: “Why do you like reading?”
How → the way: “How do you get to school?”
Always finish a question with a question mark.
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to drop the verb and write “What your name?” The question needs is: “What is your name?”
grammar.memoryTip: Wh- words are keys that unlock answers. Pick the right key: when opens time, where opens place, who opens person.
grammar.practice: Write the correct Wh- word for each question. | ______ do you have lunch? (a time) | ______ is your favourite subject? (a thing) | ______ is your best friend? (a person) | ______ do you get to school? (the way)

grammar.title: Giving Instructions (imperatives)
grammar.explanation: When we tell someone what to do, we use the imperative. It gives one clear instruction or rule, like “Respect your parents” or “Behave well in public.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the base verb and add no ending: “Listen to your teacher.”
You do not need to say “you”, because it is already understood: “Behave well” means “You behave well.”
Add please to sound polite: “Please help your friends.”
To say NOT to do something, use don't: “Don't be rude.”
grammar.commonMistake: Writing “You respects your parents” puts in a subject that is not needed. Drop the you and use the base verb: “Respect your parents.”
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the word command. An imperative gives one clear command to follow, so keep it short and strong.
grammar.practice: Complete each instruction with the base verb in brackets. Add Don't where it says (not). | ______ (listen) to your teacher. | ______ (not / disturb) others while they are working. | ______ (help) your classmates. | ______ (be) kind to everyone.

grammar.title: Action Verbs (doing words)
grammar.explanation: Action verbs tell us what someone is doing, like help, learn, run, and play. They make sentences come alive: instead of “They are”, you can say “They are helping their parents.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Find the subject first, which is the person doing it: “She is learning about health.”
Add the doing word, which is the action verb: learning, helping, running.
For he / she / it in the simple present, add -s: “He runs fast.” “She helps her mum.”
grammar.commonMistake: Many learners write “He run fast” and forget the ending. For he, she or it, add -s: “He runs fast.”
grammar.memoryTip: Action means doing. If a person is doing something, the word for that doing is an action verb.
grammar.practice: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets. | She ______ (play) the drum beautifully. | They ______ (help) their friends every day. | I ______ (learn) new words every day. | He ______ (run) fast in the race.

grammar.title: Expressing Likes and Dislikes
grammar.explanation: We talk about what we enjoy and what we do not enjoy. It helps us share our feelings, like “I like being with my family” or “He doesn't like public speaking.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use like with I, you, we and they. Use likes with he, she and it.
“I like helping my parents.”
“She likes reading books.”
To say you do NOT enjoy something, use don't for I / you / we / they, and doesn't for he / she / it.
“We don't like loud noise.”
“He doesn't like getting up late.”
grammar.commonMistake: “He like football” is missing one small letter. Add the s for he, she or it: “He likes football.”
grammar.memoryTip: I / you / we / they take like. He / she / it take likes. To say no, put not after do or does.
grammar.practice: Write like, likes, don't, or doesn't. | I ______ eating fruit with my family in the evening. | She ______ going to the park with her family. | They ______ (negative) like loud shouting in public. | He ______ (negative) like reading in a noisy room.

grammar.title: The Conjunction “and”
grammar.explanation: A conjunction is a joining word. And joins two ideas or two things together, so we can say both at once, like “Honour and duty are both important.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Join two words: “Health and safety go together.”
Join two ideas: “I help my mum and I listen to my teacher.”
In a list of three, put a comma, then and: “I like reading, drawing, and football.”
grammar.commonMistake: “I like apples I like oranges” runs two ideas together with nothing between them. Join them: “I like apples and I like oranges.”
grammar.memoryTip: Picture and as a bridge between two islands. Each island holds one idea, and the bridge joins them.
grammar.practice: Join the ideas with and. | I respect my parents ______ my teachers. | We must be kind ______ safe at school. | My family talks ______ laughs at dinner.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Introduce Your Family
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say:
“In my family, there is ______, ______, and me.”
“My ______ helps me to ______.”
“Together, my family likes to ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the people clearly? Did you speak loudly enough?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Listen to my family introduction with my helper beside me, then tell me whether every person was named clearly.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — What Do You Like?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Say these three lines out loud, filling every gap with your own idea.
“I like ______, and I like ______.”
“I don't like ______.”
“My best friend likes ______.”
Remember that he and she take likes, with an s on the end.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Check whether I said like and likes correctly, and give me one more sentence to try with my helper.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Ask Me a Question
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person asks a Wh- question: “Who is in your family?” or “What is your duty at home?”
The other person answers in a full sentence.
Then swap turns. Try to use who, what, where, when, why, and how.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Take turns with me while an adult listens, asking one Wh- question each until we have used all six question words.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Values Word Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Interview a family member about respect, honour, duty, health and safety.
Ask one question for each word, such as “How do you show respect at work?”
Record their answers, then say one sentence of your own for every word.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me the five values questions one at a time, and tell me if my answers used the word correctly.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Public or Private Role Play
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Act out two short scenes with a partner or your helper.
Scene one happens in a public place, such as the market or the school lunch area.
Scene two happens in a private place, such as your own bedroom at home.
After each scene, explain with because why you spoke that way.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Play the other person in my public scene, then ask me why my voice changed when the scene moved home.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Talk About Me
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Plan and record a one-minute talk called “All About Me and My Family”.
Open by saying your name and your age, then give three details about your family.
Finish with one value that matters to you and the reason why.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Time my one-minute talk while my helper watches, then tell me which detail I should say more about.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Simple Sentences About You
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
I like helping my ______.
In my family, I ______.
She is learning about ______.
My duty at home is to ______.
writing.modelText: In my family, I lay the table before dinner every evening. My duty at home is to keep my room tidy.
writing.successCriteria: I finished every sentence; I added two sentences of my own; I used a capital letter and a full stop each time
writing.support: Say each sentence out loud before you write it, and use the family word group for ideas.
writing.extension: Add one sentence that begins with a time phrase, such as Every morning or Later that day.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Write Some Questions
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a Wh- question you could ask a new friend about each of these:
their family → “What ______?”
how they show respect at school → “How ______?”
what they like to do → “What ______?”
writing.modelText: What is your favourite thing to do with your family? How do you show respect to your teacher?
writing.sentenceStarter: I would like to ask you
writing.successCriteria: Every question begins with a Wh- word; every question ends with a question mark; the verb is not missing
writing.support: Check the Wh- key list in Grammar Lesson 2 before you choose each question word.
writing.extension: Answer your own three questions in complete sentences, as if you were the new friend.

writing.title: Writing 3 — A Short Play Script
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a tiny play of four lines in which a junior student teaches a senior student about duty, just as Amal does in the story. Use this shape:
Junior: ______
Senior: ______
Junior: ______
Senior: ______
writing.modelText: Junior: Every morning I feed the cat, because that is my duty. Senior: Why does one small duty matter so much?
writing.successCriteria: Each line names the speaker; each line sounds like real speech; the word duty appears at least twice
writing.support: Read the play scene in Amal's Big Day again and borrow its question-and-answer shape.
writing.extension: Add two more lines in which the senior student agrees and names a duty of their own.

writing.title: Writing 4 — A Letter to a New Friend
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short letter to a new friend. Tell them about your family and about one value that matters to you, such as respect or honour. Use this shape:
Dear ______,
My name is ______. In my family there is ______.
I think ______ is important because ______.
Your friend, ______
writing.modelText: Dear Nora, my name is Amal and I live with my parents, my brothers and my sister. I think respect is important because it keeps our class calm.
writing.successCriteria: The letter has a greeting and a sign-off; the family is described; one value is explained with because
writing.support: Plan three ideas on scrap paper first: who is in your family, your value, and your reason.
writing.extension: Add a closing question that invites your new friend to write back about their own family.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Instructions
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write three instructions for one of these titles: “How to behave in public” or “How to stay safe at school”. Start every line with a base verb, like “Speak softly.” Number your lines 1, 2 and 3.
writing.modelText: 1. Walk carefully along the corridor. 2. Speak softly in the lunch area. 3. Hold the door open for younger children.
writing.successCriteria: Every line starts with a base verb; no line begins with you; the list is numbered in order
writing.support: Underline the base verbs in Grammar Lesson 3 and borrow three of them to start your lines.
writing.extension: Add one instruction that begins with Don't, and explain underneath why that rule keeps people safe.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a paragraph of six sentences about your family and the values you share. Begin with a topic sentence, then join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: My family is small but very busy. We eat dinner together and we talk about our day, because that is when we listen to each other.
writing.successCriteria: There is a topic sentence; and, but or because joins ideas at least twice; every sentence is punctuated
writing.support: Write your six ideas as short notes first, then join two pairs of them with a joining word.
writing.extension: Finish with a closing sentence that says what you would like your family to do next year.

activities.title: Activities 1 — True or False (About Values)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sentence. If it is false, write the correct sentence underneath.
1. We show respect by shouting over other people.
2. A parent is a mother or a father.
3. In a public place, we should behave well and speak softly.
4. A duty is something you must do.
activities.answerSummary: Item 1 is false and item 4 is the trickiest, because a duty is a task rather than a feeling.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Match the Word to Its Meaning
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word on the left to its meaning on the right.
1. honour → (a) being safe and not in danger
2. safety → (b) something you must do
3. duty → (c) to show someone great respect
activities.answerSummary: Learners often pair honour with safety, so ask them to say each meaning aloud before they draw a line.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill each gap with like, likes, public or private.
1. The park is a ______ place where everyone can play.
2. My diary is ______, because it is just for me.
3. I ______ helping my family.
4. She ______ reading books at night.
activities.answerSummary: Gaps 1 and 2 test the public and private pair; gaps 3 and 4 test the s ending on likes.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Present Continuous
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write what is happening now. Use is or are with the verb plus -ing.
1. The students ______ (practise) their play.
2. My family ______ (talk) about safety.
3. He ______ (help) the younger student.
activities.answerSummary: Watch for the plural subject in item 1, which needs are rather than is.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Sort all fifteen unit words into the three groups: Me and My Family, Good Behaviour and Values, and Health, Safety and Places.
Then write one sentence for each group that explains what its words have in common.
activities.answerSummary: The three group headings from the word book give the sorting rule, so check the explaining sentences most closely.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Find six details in the unit texts that show good behaviour.
Write each detail, name the text it came from, and explain what it teaches the reader.
activities.answerSummary: A complete answer quotes the text and then adds the learner's own explanation, not just the quotation.

assignments.title: All About Me and My Family learning portfolio
assignments.instructions:
Submit Writing 1 — Simple Sentences About You, Writing 4 — A Letter to a New Friend, and a recording of Speaking 1 — Introduce Your Family.
Marking guidance for the teacher: award up to 16 marks with the writing rubric and up to 16 marks with the speaking rubric.
Give full marks for writing only when every sentence is punctuated, at least five unit words are used correctly, and the letter explains one value with because.
Give full marks for speaking only when every family member is named clearly, the recording is audible throughout, and the learner answers a follow-up question in a complete sentence.
Return one improvement target per piece before the learner redrafts.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Amal lives with her parents, her brothers and her grandmother. What do we call this group of people?
quizzes.options: her market | her family | her notebook | her homework
quizzes.explanation: A family is the people who live with you and love you, so the group in the house is her family.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: In the class play, Nora acts as the older student who has learned more. What is she called?
quizzes.explanation: A senior is an older person or someone who has learned more, which is exactly the part Nora plays.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the present continuous correctly?
quizzes.options: The students are practising their lines. | The students practising their lines. | The students is practising their lines. | The students practise their lines last week.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The students are practising their lines.
quizzes.explanation: Present continuous needs a be word plus the -ing verb, and a plural subject takes are practising.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Which Wh- word asks about a time?
quizzes.explanation: When opens a time answer, while who opens a person and where opens a place.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Which sentence gives a clear instruction to a friend?
quizzes.options: You listens to your teacher. | Listen to your teacher. | Listening to your teacher. | To listen to your teacher.
quizzes.explanation: An imperative starts with the base verb and leaves out you, so Listen to your teacher is correct.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Complete this sentence: Nora ______ reading books at night.
quizzes.explanation: Nora is one person, so she takes likes with an s, just as he and it do.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from “Good Behaviour at School”: At school, we show ______ to our teacher and to our friends.
quizzes.explanation: The text opens by naming what we show to our teacher and friends, and that word is respect.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from “Amal's Big Day”: She whispered to her best friend, Nora, “What is your ______ in the play?”
quizzes.explanation: Amal asks what job Nora must take care of in the play, and a job you must do is a duty.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Where does “Public and Private” say you can play games and be loud?
quizzes.options: At home, in private. | In public, at the busy market in town. | In the lunch area at school. | At the school gate in the morning.
quizzes.explanation: The listening text pairs in private with at home, and every other option names a public place.

quizzes.quizTitle: All About Me and My Family checkpoint
quizzes.question: Which word means to show someone great respect for what they do?
quizzes.explanation: Honour is stronger than being polite, and the other three name a feeling of danger, a tool and the sky.

liveSessions.title: All About Me and My Family: Launch, vocabulary and learning goals
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read the unit overview and learn the six words in the Me and My Family group.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome and family word round; 10 min teacher models the three word groups; 15 min paired meaning match with the word book; 10 min learners say one sentence per word; 5 min goal setting for the unit
liveSessions.afterSession: Add the fifteen unit words to your word wall and tick the ones you can already use.

liveSessions.title: All About Me and My Family: Reading fluency and comprehension
liveSessions.beforeSession: Read My Family and Good Behaviour at School twice, once silently and once aloud.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min retrieval of the values words; 10 min teacher reads Amal's Big Day aloud with expression; 15 min guided evidence hunt in the two information texts; 10 min partners answer comprehension questions 1 to 4; 5 min share the best evidence found
liveSessions.afterSession: Answer comprehension questions 9 to 12 about the story in complete sentences.

liveSessions.title: All About Me and My Family: Grammar workshop and feedback
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete Grammar Lessons 1 and 2 and bring your practice answers.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min present continuous warm-up; 10 min teacher models is and are with -ing; 15 min learners rewrite Activity 4 sentences on whiteboards; 10 min Wh- question relay in pairs; 5 min correction of two common slips
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Grammar Lesson 3 on imperatives and write your three Writing 5 instructions.

liveSessions.title: All About Me and My Family: Dialogue, speaking and pronunciation
liveSessions.beforeSession: Practise Speaking 1 at home and record one attempt.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min tongue-twister warm-up with behave and behaviour; 10 min teacher models an introduction of a family; 15 min learners perform Speaking 1 in threes; 10 min likes and dislikes circle using like and likes; 5 min peer feedback on volume and clarity
liveSessions.afterSession: Re-record Speaking 1 using the two clarity points your group gave you.

liveSessions.title: All About Me and My Family: Writing conference and revision
liveSessions.beforeSession: Draft the Writing 4 letter to a new friend before the session.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min shared reading of a model letter; 10 min teacher shows how because adds a reason; 15 min individual writing conferences on the letter drafts; 10 min partners check capitals, full stops and speech marks; 5 min set one improvement target each
liveSessions.afterSession: Redraft your letter with your improvement target and check every sentence ending.

liveSessions.title: All About Me and My Family: Performance, assessment and reflection
liveSessions.beforeSession: Rehearse your four-line play script from Writing 3 with a partner.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min warm-up of public and private examples; 10 min two groups perform their play scripts; 15 min class discusses behaviour in public places with reasons; 10 min unit quiz on paper; 5 min self-assessment ticks and next steps
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete the unit quiz and the self-assessment, then choose one Not yet statement to practise.

teacherNotes.note:
Keep an adult or caregiver present for every AI tutor task and every recording in this unit, and never leave a child alone with the device.
Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, and offer spoken, written or drawn responses.
Give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision rather than general praise.
When a learner behaves differently from the story, describe the better choice instead of labelling the child.
Grammar practice answers, carried across from the Grade 3 Grammar source file:
Practice 1 (present continuous): 1. are playing. 2. is eating. 3. are learning. 4. is doing. 5. is talking.
Practice 2 (Wh- questions): 1. When. 2. What. 3. Who. 4. How.
Practice 3 (imperatives): 1. Listen. 2. Don't disturb. 3. Help. 4. Be.
Practice 4 (action verbs): 1. plays. 2. help. 3. learn. 4. runs.
Practice 5 (likes and dislikes): 1. like. 2. likes. 3. don't. 4. doesn't.
Practice 6 (the conjunction and): 1. and. 2. and. 3. and.

Vocabulary file: “Can you help me talk about the people in my family?” “What is the difference between a junior and a senior?”
Values group: “What is the difference between behave and behaviour?” “Can you check my sentence about my duty at home?”
Health and places group: “Can you quiz me on the difference between private and public?” “What can I do to keep good health?”
Reading and listening: “Can you read the family text with me and explain any hard words?” “Can you play a listening game about public and private places?”
Speaking: “Can you ask me Wh- questions about myself?” “Can you check that I say likes correctly for he and she?”
Writing: “Can you help me write my letter to a new friend?” “Can you read my play script with me, using different voices?”
Grammar: “Can you check my grammar answers?” “Why do we say he likes but I like?”
Remind learners that an adult stays beside them for every one of these prompts.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. False, because we show respect by listening quietly instead of shouting. 2. True. 3. True. 4. True.
1 goes to c, 2 goes to a, and 3 goes to b. Honour is the only one of the three that names an action towards a person.
1. public. 2. private. 3. like. 4. likes. Only gap 4 needs the s, because She is one person.
1. are practising. 2. is talking. 3. is helping. The plural subject in item 1 is the only one that takes are.
Group 1: family, parent, friend, student, junior, senior. Group 2: behave, behaviour, respect, honour, duty. Group 3: health, safety, private, public.
Accept any six details that are quoted accurately, each named to its text and followed by the learner's own explanation of what it teaches.
her family. A family is the people who live with you and love you, which is the whole household Amal lists.
senior. Nora plays the older student who has learned more, so junior is the opposite of the answer.
The students are practising their lines. The plural subject needs are, and the verb needs the -ing ending.
When. Remind the learner that who opens a person, where opens a place and how opens the way.
Listen to your teacher. An imperative starts with the base verb, so no you and no -ing.
likes. Nora can be replaced by she, and he, she and it all take the s ending.
respect. The opening sentence of the passage names what we show to our teacher and to our friends.
duty. Amal is asking which job Nora must take care of during the class play.
At home, in private. Every other option names a public place where a calm voice is expected.
honour. It means to show someone great respect, and it is the only option that names an action.

selfAssessment.statement: I can talk about the people in my family.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use words like respect, honour, and duty.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what is happening now (present continuous).
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can give instructions using imperatives.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what I like and don't like, and join ideas with “and”.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
